summary.overall: Chapter 8 was reviewed in 11 bounded source-grounded shards. Proposed changes: 177 replacements, 1 splits, and 24 removals.
patterns: Paragraph-location scaffolding is the dominant defect: 12 of 14 question stems and 18 of 18 item explanations reference specific paragraph numbers, teaching document trivia instead of operational substance.
Five true/false questions restate source content with one-sentence paragraph-citation explanations, adding no retrieval diversity beyond the MCQs on the same paragraphs.
Answer-length cues appear in 8 items where the correct answer is substantially longer or shorter than the distractors, undermining answer defensibility.
One activity (readback_check on 8-1-10) asks for a pilot readback of a controller unable message, which is not a standard readback scenario in ATC operations.
All five flashcards are clean — no paragraph references, well-scoped retrieval targets, and appropriate card types.

Paragraph-location scaffolding is universal — all 17 question stems and all 17 question explanations reference specific paragraph numbers, teaching document navigation trivia instead of operational substance.

Seven true/false explanations are thin one-sentence paragraph citations (e.g., 'That is the rule stated in 8-1-5') that provide no teaching of the controlling operational rule, condition, or exception.
Multiple-choice distractor quality is consistently good across all 10 MC questions — distractors represent plausible operational mistakes a controller in training might make.
Five of six flashcards are well-crafted: fronts clearly identify the retrieval concept, backs are concise and accurate, and card types are appropriate to the content.

The single flashcard defect in 8-2-1 packs three distinct ARTCC coordination duties into one card. The front asks about forwarding but the back adds a non-forwarding coordination-timing requirement, breaking front/back alignment.

True/false items test the same core rules as their counterpart MCQs on the same paragraphs but in a different retrieval mode (recognition vs. discrimination), providing modest cross-format reinforcement.
Paragraph-location scaffolding is the sole defect in 18 of 21 intervened items. The operational content beneath the scaffolding is sound.
True/false questions consistently pair scaffolding with paper-thin explanations that merely cite the paragraph number rather than teaching the rule.
Flashcard backs are generally concise and accurate except for one that vaguely references 'the paragraph's' conditions instead of stating them.
Explanations across all formats restate the answer without adding invented rationales, which is appropriate given the source blocks state duties and thresholds without policy justification.
Activity content objects are well-constructed: the readback_check, spot_the_error, and table_lookup activities all present operationally valid tasks with plausible distractors.
Pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding — 15 of 18 items embed paragraph numbers in stems, explanations, or activity contexts, teaching document navigation rather than operational substance.
Answer-length imbalance in 6 multiple-choice and situation-action items where the correct response is substantially longer than distractors, structurally cueing the answer.
One near-duplicate: a true/false question (032996da) restates the same 12 NM Gulf Grid System minimum already tested by an MCQ (27213cdf) at the same retrieval level.
Thin explanations in 4 items that merely cite the paragraph number or restate the answer without teaching the operational principle.
One flashcard back (ba04f994) includes extra content beyond what the front question asks, using the ambiguous phrase 'specified angle'.
Paragraph-location scaffolding pervasive across all three paragraphs: stems open with 'Under 8-X-Y' and explanations cite paragraph numbers instead of operational principles.
True/false questions (2 of 3) present the source rule nearly verbatim and test whether the student agrees, rather than testing operational application or rule conditions.
Answer-length cue appears in multiple items where the correct answer states a compound or detailed condition while distractors are brief and generic.
Flashcard front/back mismatch: one card asks about 'same-NAVAID' generically (implying both VOR and NDB) but answers only for VOR; another card has a context-free numeric back.

Activities in paragraph 8-5-1 exhibit strong cross-format coverage — the single rule is tested via knowledge_check, situation_action, and visual_interpretation — but two of four question items are low-value true/false or near-duplicates.
Paragraph-location scaffolding in stems: 6 of 9 questions and 2 of 8 activities embed paragraph numbers in their stem or situation, substituting document trivia for operational context.
Paragraph-location scaffolding in explanations: 12 of 20 items cite paragraph/subparagraph references instead of teaching the controlling operational rule directly.

Thin true/false explanations: all 3 T/F items explain answers solely by naming a paragraph letter (e.g., 'That requirement appears in 8-5-4e') rather than stating the requirement and its operational significance.
Answer-length cues: in two items the correct answer is roughly twice the word count of any distractor because it must state two conditions where each distractor states only one.
One context-light flashcard: card e757bfac uses 'Only under the listed transition conditions' as its back, which provides no retrieval value.
All distractors were defensible and operationally plausible; no manufactured phraseology errors or arbitrary frequency/callsign issues were present.
Pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding: 21 of 24 items reference paragraph numbers in stems or explanations, teaching document navigation instead of ATC substance.
Four true/false items restate the source text verbatim with answer True and thin explanations — no application, discrimination, or operational reasoning required.
Answer-length cues in 5 items where the correct answer is the longest option, particularly in situation_action activities where the correct action requires listing multiple steps.
Vague flashcards: 2 cards say 'required buffer' without specifying the quantitative minimum (one-half lateral separation minimum) that controllers must know.
Three situation_action activities open the correct answer with a negative phrase ('Do not use that shortcut...') that the distractors do not share.
Formulaic trainee-says-wrong-thing framing on situation_action activities for 8-6-1, 8-6-2, 8-6-3, and 8-7-1 — functional but repetitive across paragraphs.

Both thin paragraphs (8-7-2 and 8-8-1) carry five items each — two MCQs, two activities, and one flashcard — all testing the same single cross-reference fact, creating extreme within-paragraph duplication.
Two true/false items (cc04b4a0, e3ee8e42) provide only one-sentence paragraph-citation explanations and duplicate the MCQ on the same paragraph in the same retrieval mode.
Two knowledge-check activities (85faf193, 46a7cee6) duplicate their companion MCQs in the same retrieval mode, asking nearly identical questions with nearly identical answer sets.

Paragraph-location scaffolding is pervasive: 16 of 24 items cite paragraph numbers in stems, explanations, para_context, or situation fields, teaching document trivia rather than operational substance.

Paragraph 8-8-2 is a single cross-reference sentence but carries five items (two MCQs, a knowledge_check, a situation_action, and a flashcard) all testing the same fact — that vertical separation follows Chapter 4 IFR standards.
Two true/false items (29fda4a7 on 8-8-2, a22de225 on 8-8-5) have single-sentence paragraph-citation explanations and duplicate their companion MCQs in the same retrieval mode.
Three knowledge-check activities (5b0f5266 on 8-8-2, 61f1335a on 8-8-4d, e06bc00e on 8-8-5b1) duplicate existing MCQs — asking nearly identical questions in the same answer-selection retrieval mode.

The 8-8-3 flashcard (96261cc5) has a context-light front referencing 'the listed Caribbean-region areas' and an underspecified back of 'Use 20 minutes' that does not identify which areas or conditions the minimum applies to.

The 8-8-5 flashcard (d018d039) overloads its back with both authorization conditions (pilot request, sunrise–sunset) and post-clearance duties (alternative instructions), but omits the traffic-information duty from 8-8-5b2, creating an incomplete and jumbled retrieval target.

The table_lookup activity (8fe50bc5) using TBL 8-8-1, the 23 NM lateral flashcard (a76813bf), and the 8-8-2 vertical separation flashcard (922c6bb2) are clean — well-scoped retrieval targets with proper source identification.

Redundant true/false items: four T/F questions restate the source paragraph as a proposition whose answer is always 'True', adding no retrieval value beyond the companion multiple-choice questions and activities that test the same concept.
Thin explanations: several explanations restate the answer or the source cross-reference without articulating the operational condition, exception, or decision principle.

Paragraph-suffix notation in flashcard back: one flashcard uses 'Section 8-9' instead of the correct FAA designation 'Section 9'.
Situation-action para_context fields consistently carry 'Under 8-9-X' prefixes that serve no operational purpose in the activity flow.

Paragraph-location scaffolding is the dominant defect across all four paragraphs — stems lead with 'Under 8-10-X,' explanations say 'Per 8-10-X,' and one flashcard back includes the chapter-and-section number.
Thin explanations restate the answer in slightly different words without teaching the operational context or contrasting conditions.
Three items are near-duplicates: T/F questions that restate the same rule tested by an existing multiple-choice question, and a knowledge_check activity that mirrors a question on the same paragraph.
Answer-length cue in one situation_action activity — the correct answer is substantially longer than all distractors.
The table_lookup activity for TBL 8-10-1 is well-constructed with proper source identification, specific lookup parameters, and plausible distractors.

generation_guidance: Never embed paragraph numbers in question stems or explanations; students must learn the rule, not where to find it.

True/false questions must teach the underlying principle in the explanation, not merely cite the source paragraph. Reserve T/F format for concepts that benefit from binary discrimination and are not already covered by an MCQ on the same paragraph.
Balance distractor length to match the correct answer length. A conspicuously short or long correct choice reveals the answer regardless of content.

Match activity type to the skill being tested. Readback-check activities should test actual clearance readbacks, not verbatim repetition of advisory or unable messages. For weather deviation scenarios, use situation_action or phraseology-recognition formats.
Avoid redundant T/F items when an MCQ already covers the same paragraph's core rule. Each item should retrieve a distinct operational concept.
When the source paragraph provides a single integrated rule (e.g., 8-1-1), one MCQ plus cross-format reinforcement via flashcard and one activity is sufficient coverage without duplication.
For dense procedural paragraphs like 8-1-10, distribute items across the decision tree branches so each tests a distinct operational contingency rather than restating the same final step.
Never embed paragraph numbers in stems or explanations. Replace 'Under 8-1-5' with operational context such as 'In oceanic control areas' or 'When applying oceanic separation.'

True/false explanations must teach the controlling operational rule, condition, or exception. 'That is the rule stated in 8-1-5' is a citation, not an explanation. State what the controller must do, when, and under what conditions.

Align flashcard fronts and backs precisely. If the front asks what must be forwarded to ATS facilities, the back must list only forwardable items. If the concept is broader, adjust the front to match.
For multi-requirement paragraphs like 8-2-1, create one flashcard per distinct operational duty rather than combining several into a single overloaded card.

Exact numeric recall targets — 29.92 inches Hg for oceanic altimeter setting, 10 minutes for missing position report action, 3 minutes for estimate revision forwarding — are appropriate for flashcard and true/false retrieval.

When a source paragraph is a single sentence (8-1-5, 8-1-6), one MCQ plus one flashcard provides sufficient coverage. A T/F on the same paragraph should test a distinct sub-element or be structured as a negative-discrimination item testing a common misconception.

Avoid cross-referencing other paragraph numbers (e.g., 'paragraph 2-1-29') in stems and explanations. When the source content IS a cross-reference, rephrase to state the operational requirement directly rather than the pointer to it.
Never open a question stem with 'Under 8-X-Y' or any paragraph identifier. State the operational scenario or concept directly.
Write explanations that teach the controlling requirement without citing paragraph numbers. The explanation should repeat the rule in plain language, not just state where it appears.

Ensure all distractors in multiple-choice questions are comparable in length to the correct answer. When the correct answer lists multiple conditions, distill it to its operational essence rather than reproducing a compound sentence.

For flashcards, state the retrieval target in the back without referencing document structure ('the paragraph,' 'the section'). If the back would exceed roughly 60 words, consider whether the concept should be split.

Cross-format reinforcement exists between some question/flashcard pairs. When generating new content, prioritize uncovered source statements over additional formats for already-covered ones unless the existing coverage is weak.

Table and figure activities should name the source (e.g., TBL 8-3-1, FIG 8-3-1) in the source_label field but reference the figures by their operational content (not paragraph number) in question_text.
Never include paragraph numbers in question stems, explanations, activity contexts, or visual-interpretation question text. Make every item self-contained.
Balance answer choice lengths across each multiple-choice and situation-action item so the correct answer does not stand out by verbosity, specificity, or structure.
Do not generate true/false items that duplicate the exact fact tested by an existing MCQ in the same chapter section. Prefer the MCQ format for numeric minima and rule conditions.
Ensure flashcard back text directly answers the front's question without extraneous content. If the widening rule is a separate retrieval target, create a dedicated card for it.

Explanations must restate the operational rule from the source in self-contained terms, not merely cite a paragraph number or repeat the answer verbatim. Include the full rule sufficient for the student to understand the controlling principle.

Never embed paragraph numbers in stems, question_text, or explanations. The stem must be self-contained with the operational scenario; the paragraph identity belongs only in the source_basis field used by the remediation system, not in student-facing content.

Avoid true/false items that simply restate the rule. Convert them to multiple-choice items testing conditions, exceptions, or operational application, or remove them when the concept is already covered by other items.

Balance distractor length against correct-answer length. When the correct answer states a compound condition, inflate distractors with parallel structure and specific (but wrong) operational details so length does not reveal the answer.

Scope flashcard fronts precisely. A card asking about 'same-NAVAID track separation' must address both VOR/VORTAC/TACAN (15-degree divergence) and NDB (30-degree divergence plus inbound restriction), or the front must name the specific NAVAID type.

Flashcard backs must contain enough context for reverse retrieval. A back that reads only '11 miles' or 'True' cannot function as a reverse prompt. Include the operational parameter or at minimum the category of the answer.

For paragraphs with a single rule and limited source material, limit items to distinct retrieval angles rather than piling on near-duplicates. One paragraph produced four items (two questions, one activity, one flashcard) covering the same single rule from angles that became indistinguishable.

Never embed paragraph numbers in stems, explanations, or situation contexts. Use self-contained operational descriptions instead (e.g., 'During the offshore-to-oceanic transition' rather than 'Under 8-5-3').
True/false explanations must teach the requirement, not just name a paragraph letter. State what the rule is and, when helpful, how it fits within the broader set of conditions.

When a correct answer must state multiple conditions, match distractor length by elaborating distractors with operationally plausible but incorrect rationale, or restructure into focused single-condition items.

Flashcard backs must be substantive. A phrase like 'under the listed transition conditions' teaches nothing. Either name the key condition domains or split into multiple cards if the procedure has too many conditions for one back.
Do not add safety rationale, purpose statements, or system-design claims that are absent from the source blocks. State only what the procedure requires.

Paragraph 8-5-5 (radar identification application) has eight conditions plus an exception — it is the most complex paragraph in this shard and deserves proportionally more retrieval coverage across formats.
Never embed paragraph numbers in question stems or activity situation text. Write stems that stand alone using the operational concept.
Explanations must teach the controlling rule, not cite paragraph numbers. State what the rule requires directly.

Avoid true/false items that merely restate a source sentence verbatim. Convert to multiple-choice with plausible operational distractors, or remove if the concept is adequately covered by another item.

Balance answer choice lengths so the correct answer is not the conspicuously longest option. For situation_action items, express the correct action in the fewest words that capture all required steps.
Use positive phrasing in correct-answer choices. Avoid leading with 'Do not...' or 'Never...' unless exclusion is the actual skill being tested.
Flashcard backs must state quantitative thresholds explicitly (e.g., 'one-half the lateral separation minimum') rather than using placeholder words like 'required buffer.'
Flashcards must avoid paragraph-location content on either side. Teach the operational rule, not where in the document to find it.
Knowledge_check activity explanations should connect choices to the controlling principle, not merely restate the correct answer prefixed by a paragraph citation.

For paragraph 8-7-1, ensure items teach the governing rule (regional procedures apply) without reproducing the self-referential 'this section' phrasing that is ambiguous in a standalone learning context.
Never embed paragraph numbers in stems, explanations, or para_context fields. The student must learn the rule, condition, or threshold, not where to find it in the Order.

For thin enabling paragraphs that contain only a cross-reference or single governance statement, produce at most one question plus cross-format reinforcement via a flashcard and one operational activity (situation_action or scenario). Five items on a single sentence is harmful duplication.

True/false items must have explanations that teach the underlying operational principle, not merely cite the source paragraph. Reserve T/F format for binary discrimination concepts not already covered by an MCQ on the same paragraph.

Avoid knowledge-check activities that duplicate an existing MCQ. A knowledge_check should test a different retrieval angle — a lookup decision, a conditional threshold, or an operational comparison — not the same fact with the same choice structure.

When a paragraph has multiple subparagraphs with distinct minima (e.g., 8-7-4 a–e), distribute items across the different standards so each operational threshold is tested. Do not concentrate four items on a single subparagraph while leaving the others uncovered.

Balance distractor length to match the correct answer. For multi-condition items where the correct choice is unavoidably longer, add substantive detail to distractors referencing other paragraph provisions so length alone does not reveal the answer.

Explanations must teach the controlling rule, condition, or exception. Do not invent safety rationales, operational purposes, or system-design justifications that the source blocks do not state. State the rule directly and note what distinguishes it from the alternatives.

For situation_action activities, frame the situation around an operational decision point where the trainee's incorrect assumption drives the conflict. The correct action should require recognizing which rule applies, not which paragraph to consult.
Never embed paragraph numbers in stems, explanations, para_context, or situation fields. The student must learn the rule, threshold, or operational condition — not where to find it in the Order.

For thin enabling paragraphs that contain only a cross-reference or single governance statement (e.g., 8-8-2), produce at most one question plus cross-format reinforcement via a flashcard and a situation_action activity. Five items on a single sentence is harmful duplication.

When a paragraph has multiple subparagraphs with distinct minima (e.g., 8-8-3 a–f), distribute items across the different standards so each operational threshold is tested. Do not concentrate items on a single subparagraph while leaving others uncovered.

Balance distractor length to match the correct answer. For multi-condition items where the correct choice is unavoidably longer, either add substantive detail to distractors or refocus the question on a single condition from the list.

Flashcard backs must be complete retrieval targets. A front referencing vague locations or a back listing only a bare number without conditions is underspecified. Front and back together must define the full operational rule.

Flashcards must not merge authorization conditions with post-clearance duties in a single back. Split items that cover distinct operational steps (e.g., when an action may be performed vs. what must be done after).

Explanations must teach the controlling rule, condition, or threshold. Do not invent safety rationales or operational purposes the source blocks do not state. State the rule directly and note what distinguishes it from the alternatives.
Do not prefix question stems with 'Under X-X-X' or any paragraph citation. The stem must be self-contained using operational context alone.
Do not begin explanations with 'Per 8-9-X' or 'stated in 8-9-X.' Explain the controlling rule, condition, or operational trigger without citing paragraph numbers.
Avoid true/false questions that restate a source sentence as a proposition. True/false is only appropriate when the student must evaluate a non-obvious operational claim against the rule.
Balance choice lengths: when the correct answer enumerates multiple conditions, write distractors of comparable detail so answer length does not reveal correctness.
Use standard FAA section designations (e.g., 'Section 9') not paragraph-suffix notation ('Section 8-9') when a section reference is operationally necessary.
For situation_action activities, do not place paragraph citations in the para_context field. State the operational rule directly as context for the evaluator.
Do not duplicate the same concept across a multiple-choice question and a true/false question within the same paragraph unless the T/F tests a genuinely distinct recognition task.
Do not prefix stems with 'Under 8-10-X' or 'Per paragraph X' — embed the operational context directly in the stem.
Do not begin explanations with 'Per 8-10-X' — state the controlling rule, condition, or exception without citing the paragraph number.
Ensure explanations are at least 10 words and teach the operational principle, not merely restate the answer. Contrast related minima or conditions when the source provides them.
Avoid creating both a multiple-choice question and a true/false question on the same single-sentence paragraph — one format is sufficient.

When the source paragraph covers multiple distinct minima (e.g., 50 NM, 23 NM, 90 NM), ensure each minimum is tested by a distinct item rather than creating both a question and an activity on the same minimum.
Keep situation_action correct answers concise; trim to the operational decision without restating every condition from the source.
Flashcard backs should state the operational content, not the section or chapter number where it appears.
Para_context fields in situation_action activities should summarize the controlling rule without paragraph-location scaffolding.
shard_overviews: Chapter 8 Shard 1 covers five introductory paragraphs (8-1-1 through 8-1-4 and 8-1-10) establishing oceanic and offshore ATC service rules, weather deviation procedures, VFR flight plan constraints, and separation types. The questions and activities are generally source-faithful, but the shard suffers from pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding across nearly every question and explanation. Five true/false questions provide only thin paragraph-citation explanations and are redundant with the multiple-choice items covering the same paragraphs. Several items exhibit answer-length cues where the correct choice is conspicuously shorter or longer than distractors. One readback-check activity mismatches format to skill by asking for a pilot readback of a controller unable-to-clear transmission. Activities are otherwise well-constructed with plausible distractors and operational scenarios. All five flashcards are sound and require no intervention.

Chapter 8 Shard 2 covers six paragraphs (8-1-5 through 8-2-1) across oceanic altimeter setting, position-report timing, error-report procedures, control estimates, RVSM operations, and ARTCC coordination requirements. All 17 questions and 6 flashcards are source-faithful with no factually incorrect content. However, the shard exhibits universal paragraph-location scaffolding: every question stem begins with 'Under 8-1-X' or 'Under 8-2-1' and every question explanation references the source paragraph by number. The seven true/false questions compound this with thin explanations that name the source paragraph instead of teaching the operational rule. One flashcard from 8-2-1 packs three distinct ARTCC duties into a single back with a front that asks about 'forwarding' while the back mixes forwarding and non-forwarding requirements. The remaining five flashcards are well-formed with clear retrieval targets. No activities are present in the shard. All 17 questions and 1 flashcard require replacement.

All 24 items in this shard teach genuine ATC operational substance drawn from the source paragraphs. No items are factually wrong, educationally harmful, or redundant within a single format. The dominant defect is pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding: stems open with 'Under 8-X-Y,' explanations state 'Per 8-X-Y,' and one flashcard back references 'the paragraph's' conditions. Two items have additionally thin explanations, and one multiple-choice question has a correct answer noticeably longer than its distractors. All interventions are straightforward scaffolding removals and explanation rewrites; no content needs to be removed or split.

This shard covers three concise paragraphs (8-4-1, 8-4-2, 8-4-3) on lateral separation application, separation methods, and route protected airspace reduction. Content fidelity to the source blocks is high — source facts are accurately represented across all three formats. The most widespread defect is paragraph-location scaffolding: virtually every stem, explanation, and situation context references a paragraph number instead of being self-contained. Several items exhibit answer-length cues where the correct choice is structurally longer or more detailed than distractors. One true/false question directly duplicates an existing MCQ testing the same Gulf Grid System minimum. Two flashcards and one activity are well-constructed and require no intervention. Explanations range from adequate operational restatements to thin paragraph-number citations.

The shard covers three well-defined separation rules (track separation, altitude transition, course divergence) with mostly accurate content. The dominant defect is paragraph-location scaffolding: nearly every item frontloads 'Under 8-X-Y' or embeds paragraph numbers in explanations, teaching document structure instead of ATC substance. Two of three true/false items present the rule verbatim and ask whether it is correct — a format that tests reading recognition rather than operational understanding. One flashcard scopes 'same-NAVAID' generically but answers only for VOR/VORTAC/TACAN, creating a silent miscue for the NDB case. Another flashcard has a one-word back that fails reverse-card use. Several items exhibit answer-length cues where the correct choice is substantially longer than distractors. Five items (two activities, two flashcards, one table-lookup activity) were kept as-is after careful review.

This shard covers three procedural paragraphs (8-5-3 opposite-direction, 8-5-4 same-direction, 8-5-5 radar-identification application). Source fidelity is strong across all 20 items — no factual errors were found. Every item correctly represents its governing source block. The primary defect is pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding: most stems and explanations embed 'Under 8-5-X' or 'Per 8-5-Xb' references instead of self-contained operational context. Three true/false items have explanations that only cite a paragraph letter without teaching the requirement. Two items exhibit answer-length cues where the correct choice is substantially longer than distractors. One flashcard is context-light, relying on 'under the listed transition conditions' as a back that teaches nothing. No items require removal or splitting — all 16 interventions are targeted replacements preserving the original retrieval goal.

Shard quality is moderate. Source fidelity is high — no item contains a factual error. The dominant defect is pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding: nearly every stem opens with "Under 8-X-Y" and nearly every explanation begins "Per 8-X-Y," teaching document coordinates instead of operational substance. Four true/false items are verbatim restatements of source text with answer True, offering no cognitive engagement. Answer-length cues are present where the correct answer is the longest choice, especially in situation_action activities. Two flashcards use the vague phrase "the required buffer" instead of the quantitative minimum the source states. Three situation_action activities use the formulaic trainee-says-wrong-thing pattern with negatively phrased correct answers. The 8-7-1 flashcard teaches section-number trivia on its back. The visual_interpretation activity for 8-6-1 is well constructed with no defects. The 8-6-2 procedure flashcard is accurate and well-focused.

Shard 8 covers four paragraphs (8-7-2 through 8-8-1) addressing North Atlantic vertical, longitudinal, and lateral separation, plus Caribbean ICAO Region service governance. Paragraphs 8-7-3 and 8-7-4 are procedurally dense and support varied item coverage; paragraphs 8-7-2 and 8-8-1 are single-sentence cross-references or enabling clauses yet carry five items each, nearly all testing the same fact. The shard has pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding in stems, explanations, and situation contexts. Two true/false items on thin paragraphs add no retrieval diversity beyond their companion MCQs. The 8-7-4 item set overweights 23 NM lateral separation (four of six items) while 50 NM, 60 NM, and 120 NM standards are unrepresented. Two knowledge-check activities duplicate their companion MCQs in the same retrieval mode. The table_lookup activity, three flashcards, and two of the situation_action activities are well-constructed and need no intervention.

Shard 9 covers four paragraphs (8-8-2 through 8-8-5) on Caribbean ICAO Region separation standards. Paragraph 8-8-3 is procedurally dense with longitudinal separation subparagraphs a–f, two ADS-C/B note blocks, and TBL 8-8-1, and supports the most varied item coverage. Paragraph 8-8-2 is a single-sentence cross-reference yet carries five items — two MCQs, two activities, and a flashcard — all testing the same fact. Paragraph 8-8-5 has three items testing a single duty (issue alternative instructions) across an MCQ, T/F, and knowledge_check. Paragraph-location scaffolding is the dominant defect, appearing in 16 of 24 item stems, explanations, or context fields. Two true/false items (29fda4a7, a22de225) on thin paragraphs add no retrieval diversity beyond their companion MCQs. Four knowledge-check activities (5b0f5266, 61f1335a, e06bc00e, and the duplicated MCQ in shard) test the same fact as existing questions in the same retrieval mode. The table_lookup activity, the 23 NM lateral flashcard, and the 8-8-2 vertical separation flashcard are well-constructed and need no intervention.

This shard covers Pacific ICAO Region procedures across 8-9-1 through 8-9-4. Source fidelity to the provided blocks is generally correct — minima, table values, and cross-references match the source. However, the content-generation pass introduced pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding in question stems and explanations. Four true/false questions across the four paragraphs are verbatim source restatements that test text recognition rather than operational application and are redundant with companion multiple-choice questions or activities. Several items exhibit answer-length cues or thin explanations that merely repeat the answer without teaching the controlling rule or condition. The table-lookup activity and most flashcards are well-constructed and require no intervention.

This shard covers four short paragraphs establishing North American ICAO Region procedural authority and separation minima. Source fidelity is intact — all items correctly reflect the source material. The primary defect is pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding: stems begin with 'Under 8-10-X,' explanations cite paragraph numbers, and flashcard backs contain section numbers. Three near-duplicate items test the same content in a second format without adding retrieval value. One activity has an answer-length cue. After remediation, all 23 items are accounted for: 16 replacements clean paragraph-location patterns and improve explanation depth, 3 removals eliminate redundant true/false and knowledge_check duplicates, and 4 items are retained as-is.

operations.problem: Stem references paragraph number with 'Under 8-1-1,' teaching paragraph-location trivia instead of operational substance. Explanation also cites the paragraph number. The correct answer is the shortest choice, creating a mild answer-length cue.
operations.source_basis: Provide air traffic control service in oceanic controlled airspace in accordance with the procedures in this chapter except when other procedures/minima are prescribed in a directive or a letter of agreement.

match.question_text: Under 8-1-1, how is ATC service provided in oceanic controlled airspace?

replacements.question_text: How is ATC service provided in oceanic controlled airspace?
replacements.explanation: ATC service in oceanic controlled airspace is provided using the procedures in Chapter 8. When a directive or letter of agreement prescribes different procedures or minima for a specific operation, those supersede the default Chapter 8 rule.

choices.text: Using Chapter 8 procedures, except when a directive or letter of agreement prescribes different procedures or minima for the operation.
Only according to local facility directives, since Chapter 8 does not govern oceanic controlled airspace.
Only according to ICAO procedures, with JO 7110.65 applying solely to domestic en route airspace.
Only when a letter of agreement has been signed and distributed for the specific route or sector.

content.para_context: Under the same-direction climb-through procedure, a 5-minute minimum may be used while a following aircraft climbs through the altitude of a preceding aircraft only if the preceding aircraft is level at the assigned altitude and maintaining a speed equal to or greater than the following aircraft, 5 minutes minimum exists, the following aircraft is separated by not more than 4,000 feet when the climb clearance is issued, the climb begins within 10 minutes after passing the required point or radar-observed position of the preceding aircraft, and the following aircraft remains in direct communication with ATC until vertical separation is established.

choices.text: Do not use the climb-through procedure yet, because the following aircraft must be no more than 4,000 feet below the preceding aircraft when the climb clearance is issued.
Issue the climb because the 5-minute spacing alone is sufficient as long as both aircraft remain on the same course.
Issue the climb because the altitude-difference limit applies only after the aircraft enter oceanic airspace, not during the transition.
Issue the climb because the direct-communication requirement replaces the 4,000-foot requirement once the preceding aircraft is level.

content.explanation: The same-direction climb-through procedure requires that the following aircraft be separated by not more than 4,000 feet from the preceding aircraft when the climb clearance is issued. In this situation the following aircraft is 5,000 feet below — exceeding the maximum allowed difference — so the procedure cannot yet be applied even though the speed, spacing, and communication conditions are all satisfied.

Reduce the spacing to 1 degree latitude because the automation outage allows any reduced lateral standard to be substituted.

choices.text: Do not use 23 NM until all required conditions are met, including the maintained and monitored ADS-C contracts by the automated flight data processor.

content.explanation: The 23 NM lateral separation standard requires all specified conditions: approved aircraft meeting RNP 4/RCP 240/RSP 180, designated airspace, direct controller/pilot communications, and ADS-C contracts maintained and monitored by an automated flight data processor. choices.text: 20 minutes.
15 minutes.
30 minutes.
10 minutes.

choices.text: Do not approve the ITP climb yet, because the reported ITP distance to each reference aircraft must be 15 NM or more before the clearance may be issued.

The clearance may not be issued until all conditions, including the 15 NM distance, are met.

choices.text: Do not use the reduced lateral separation while the aircraft has degraded track-keeping capability; apply another authorized lateral minimum instead.
Continue the reduced minimum because communications are established and the aircraft is still generally on track.
Keep the reduced minimum as long as the pilot accepts responsibility for navigation accuracy.

The restriction is unconditional — it applies regardless of whether communications are maintained or the aircraft appears on course. The controller must apply another authorized lateral minimum instead.
